title: From correlation to a causal claim

objectives.statement: Classify a study conclusion as association, mechanism, bounded causal attribution, or unsupported from the described evidence.
Use time order and causal arrow maps to distinguish confounders, reverse causation, mediators, and colliders and to judge whether adjustment serves the stated causal question.
Justify the strongest warranted claim from a randomized, observational, or natural-experiment comparison while stating its identifying assumptions and a material scope boundary.

scenes.title: The same difference can support different claims
scenes.contentHtml:
The same difference can support different claims

Two research summaries land in your inbox.

  • In one course, students who chose to use a flashcard app scored eight points higher than students who did not.
  • In another course, eligible students were randomly offered the app or offered it after the final exam. The first group scored three points higher on average.

Both summaries report a difference in scores. They do not answer the same scientific question. The first tells you that app use and scores travelled together in that group. The second may tell you what changed because access was offered, provided the assignment and follow-up were carried out well.

That gap between “travelled together” and “changed because of” is the work of this lesson. You will learn to judge three kinds of conclusion:

  1. Association: what varies with what?
  2. Mechanism: what process could carry a change from a proposed cause to an outcome, and what evidence supports that process?
  3. Causal attribution: compared with a relevant alternative, what would change if the exposure were changed?

The point is not to repeat “correlation is not causation” and stop. The useful skill is deciding what a particular design does support, what rival explanation remains, and how narrow the conclusion must be.

Check
id check-prerequisite-design

Before reading on, answer from memory:

  1. In each summary, who determined whether a student had app access: the student, the researcher, or an outside rule?
  2. Which summary is observational, and which is experimental?
  3. Name the exposure and outcome in both summaries.

Pause before checking the routing feedback below.

If you identified self-selection in the first summary and researcher random assignment in the second, your study-design prerequisite is ready. In both summaries, the exposure is app access or use and the outcome is the exam score. If those distinctions were uncertain, use the recovery route before continuing; the rest of the lesson builds on them.

Source Note
claims claim-inference-targets, claim-causal-contrast, claim-randomization-scopesources source-causal-diagrams, source-natural-experiments, source-randomized-trials, source-mechanistic-evidence

The cited causal-inference and mechanistic-evidence sources support the distinction among observed association, evidence about a process, and a bounded contrast under intervention. They also support treating randomization as a design for comparison rather than as a guarantee that every realized group is identical.

Recovery route

Review the published lesson Choosing a study design: assignment, selection, and time. Focus on who creates the comparison, then return and classify the two summaries again. You may also use this three-line check:

  • Researcher assigns exposure: experiment.
  • People or circumstances determine exposure: observational study.
  • An outside rule or event determines exposure: natural-experiment candidate whose assignment process still needs checking.

This route is non-punitive. Do not continue until you can identify exposure, outcome, and assignment without relying on the study's label.

Scope for this lesson

You will judge causal language at an introductory level. You will not estimate formal causal models, perform multivariable regression, prove an instrumental variable valid, or decompose direct and indirect effects. Those tasks need additional assumptions and methods. This lesson is educational and does not establish clinical advice, professional authorization, or the safety or effectiveness of any intervention.

scenes.title: Ask what the evidence is trying to prove
scenes.contentHtml:
Ask what the evidence is trying to prove

A result becomes easier to judge when you first name the question it is meant to answer. “Are app use and scores related?” is not the same question as “Would offering the app change scores?” or “Through what learning process might an effect occur?”

Three targets of inference

Target Question A conclusion with the right shape
Association Do measured values of XX and YY differ together in the sampled data? “App users scored higher on average in this cohort.”
Mechanism What entities, activities, and ordered changes could connect XX to YY, and which links have evidence? “The evidence supports a route from reminders to retrieval sessions to later recall.”
Causal attribution How would the outcome differ under a specified change in XX versus a relevant alternative? “Randomly offering access increased the mean score by three points in the studied group under these conditions.”

Here, XX names the exposure or intervention and YY names the outcome. An association is a property of observed data. A causal effect is a contrast between outcomes under alternatives—for example, app access offered now versus after the exam. The same student cannot experience both alternatives at the same moment, so a design must create a comparison group that can stand in for the unobserved alternative.

That is why the assignment process matters. Random assignment makes the assigned groups comparable in expectation before the intervention. A single randomization can still produce chance imbalances, and problems after assignment—unequal loss to follow-up, different outcome measurement, one group's treatment affecting the other group, or analyzing people only by what they chose to do—can weaken the comparison. A well-conducted trial therefore supports a bounded effect of assignment in the studied sample; it does not automatically establish an effect in every population or an effect of actually receiving the intervention.

An observational comparison starts in a different place. Because exposure was not randomized, the observed groups may differ for reasons that also affect the outcome. Observational data can contribute to causal inference, but only when a design and explicit assumptions make the comparison defensible. “We adjusted for many variables” is not such a defense by itself.

Source Note
claims claim-inference-targets, claim-causal-contrast, claim-randomization-scopesources source-causal-diagrams, source-natural-experiments, source-randomized-trials, source-mechanistic-evidence

The sources support defining a causal question as a contrast under specified alternatives, treating mechanism evidence as evidence about the connecting process, and limiting randomized estimates to their actual assignment, implementation, and population conditions.

A third variable has a job, not just a correlation

Suppose a third variable ZZ is related to both XX and YY. That fact does not tell you whether to adjust for ZZ. Its causal position and timing do.

  • A confounder is a pre-exposure common cause on an open noncausal path: X←Z→YX \leftarrow Z \rightarrow Y. Prior preparation may influence both choosing the app and the later score. If that path is ignored, part or all of the app–score association may be due to preparation. Adjustment can block a correctly identified and measured path, but unmeasured or poorly measured common causes can leave residual confounding.
  • A mediator is on a proposed causal route: X→Z→YX \rightarrow Z \rightarrow Y. Retrieval sessions might carry part of an effect of assigned access to the score. Adjusting for the mediator is not ordinary confounding control; it changes the question from the total effect to an effect that excludes or fixes that route, and valid mediation analysis needs further assumptions.
  • A collider is a common effect: X→Z←YX \rightarrow Z \leftarrow Y or, more generally, a common effect of two causes you are comparing. App use and seeking extra support may both affect who appears in a help-session sample. Restricting or adjusting for that common effect can create an association between its causes even if none existed before selection.

Reverse causation is a different direction problem: YY influences XX. If current difficulty prompts students to start using the app, a snapshot association cannot tell whether app use preceded the difficulty. Time order rules out some arrows, but time order alone does not rule out common causes.

The diagram is a thinking tool, not a discovery machine. Its arrows are scientific assumptions that need evidence and may be wrong. Draw the smallest map needed for the question, then ask which paths the design blocks, opens, or leaves uncertain.

Source Note
claims claim-third-variable-rolessources source-causal-diagrams

The causal-diagram source supports the fork, chain, and collider structures, the risk of reverse direction, and the warning that adjustment depends on a defensible causal structure rather than on statistical association alone.

Mechanism evidence answers “how,” not “how much everywhere”

A mechanism claim names the relevant parts, what they do, their order, and the conditions needed for the process. In the app example, a proposed mechanism might be:

assigned reminder → learner attempts retrieval → memory is updated → later test response

Evidence for one or more links can make the process more credible. Manipulating a reminder and measuring the next step can support a local causal link. Observing the intermediate after exposure can be consistent with the mechanism. Neither result, by itself, estimates the app's effect on every course outcome or population. Conversely, a randomized outcome difference can support causal attribution even when the full mechanism is still uncertain.

Mechanism and difference-making evidence are therefore complementary. Keep the claims separate before integrating them.

Source Note
claims claim-mechanism-evidencesources source-mechanistic-evidence

The mechanistic-evidence source supports distinguishing evidence about the existence or details of a connecting process from an outcome comparison, and it explains why mechanism evidence helps interpretation and generalization without replacing a direct effect estimate.

scenes.title: Audit three evidence packets about one app
scenes.contentHtml:
Audit three evidence packets about one app

This worked example is fictional. Its numbers were created for instruction; they are not a report about a real app or a claim about learning effectiveness.

Worked Example
id worked-example-app-evidencelabel From observed difference to calibrated claim

Task. A course team asks, “Does offering this flashcard app improve the final-exam score, and if so, how might it work?” Judge the strongest conclusion supported by each packet of evidence.

Packet Design and result
A: ordinary cohort Of 580 students, those who chose the app averaged 81 and nonusers averaged 73. Before app use, the two groups' prior-course averages were 84 and 76, respectively.
B: mechanism study In a separate 60-person laboratory task, participants were randomly assigned to receive or not receive a scheduling reminder. The reminder group initiated more retrieval sessions before a delayed recall task. The study did not measure a course exam.
C: randomized course study Six hundred eligible students were randomly offered immediate app access or access after the final. The immediate-offer group averaged 77 and the delayed-offer group 74 on the same anonymously graded exam. Seven percent of assigned students in each group lacked an exam score.

Represent the question. Let XX be the immediate offer of app access, MM be app-mediated retrieval sessions, and YY be the final-exam score. The causal contrast of interest is the mean score if all eligible students were offered access now versus the mean score if all were offered access after the final. Prior preparation CC could influence both self-selected use and the score.

Plan. For each packet:

  1. identify how XX was assigned and whether XX preceded YY;
  2. locate a plausible common cause, reverse arrow, mediator, or selection step;
  3. match the conclusion to association, mechanism, or causal attribution;
  4. state what the conclusion is about and where it stops.

Packet A. App users scored eight points higher in this cohort. That is an association. The prior-course difference is evidence that the groups were already unlike one another before app use. Prior preparation is a plausible common cause of app choice and final score, so the crude eight-point contrast cannot be attributed to app use. Measuring preparation does not prove that every common cause was measured or controlled well. The strongest short conclusion is:

In this cohort, self-selected app use was associated with a higher mean score; the comparison does not isolate an effect of app use.

Packet B. The randomized reminder isolates a causal effect of the reminder on the measured intermediate—starting retrieval sessions—in this laboratory task, assuming the randomization and measurement were carried out as described. It supplies evidence for one link in a proposed mechanism. It does not measure the app offer's effect on a course exam, so it cannot answer the whole effectiveness question. The strongest conclusion is:

The laboratory result supports the reminder-to-retrieval link in a proposed mechanism under the task conditions; it does not estimate an effect on course scores.

Packet C. Random assignment creates comparable offer groups in expectation, the offer precedes the exam, and both groups take the same anonymously graded outcome. The difference in means is 77−74=377 - 74 = 3 points. An intention-to-treat comparison keeps students in their assigned offer groups whether or not each one used the app. Under the stated implementation and missing-outcome conditions, the strongest conclusion is:

Offering immediate access increased the mean exam score by three points compared with delayed access among eligible students in this course.

That sentence attributes an effect to the offer, not to actual use. It also stays within the studied course and conditions. Generalizing to other subjects, assessment types, access arrangements, or populations needs additional evidence.

Independent checks. The eight-point observational contrast is much larger than the three-point randomized-offer contrast. That does not prove the cohort was confounded, but it is consistent with positive self-selection into app use. Equal missing-score percentages do not prove missingness was harmless; investigators would still inspect why scores were missing and whether missingness differed in relevant ways. If 30% of one assigned group but 5% of the other lacked outcomes, attrition would become a serious rival explanation.

Interpret all three together. Packet A detects an association worth explaining. Packet B supports one mechanistic link. Packet C supports a bounded causal attribution for the offer. Combining packets can give a richer account, but one packet cannot silently inherit the strengths of another.

Self-explanation. Which single design feature changes the score comparison from association by default in Packet A to bounded causal attribution in Packet C? Why does that feature justify a claim about the offer rather than a claim about app use by every student?

Source Note
claims claim-inference-targets, claim-causal-contrast, claim-third-variable-roles, claim-randomization-scope, claim-mechanism-evidencesources source-causal-diagrams, source-natural-experiments, source-randomized-trials, source-mechanistic-evidence

The sources support the worked audit's reasoning structure: define the causal contrast, identify assignment and alternative paths, keep effects of assignment distinct from actual receipt, separate mechanism evidence from outcome-effect evidence, and limit any conclusion to the population and conditions studied. All study details and numbers in the example are original fictional teaching material.

scenes.title: Do not adjust by reflex
scenes.contentHtml:
Do not adjust by reflex

Before looking back, reconstruct the core model on paper or in plain text.

Check
id check-reconstruct-causal-judgment
  1. Complete the sentence: an association asks whether two variables vary together, whereas a causal contrast asks ______.
  2. Draw the three arrow shapes for a confounder, mediator, and collider.
  3. Explain why random assignment makes groups comparable in expectation but not necessarily identical in one realized experiment.

Pause and commit to an answer before reading the feedback.

A causal contrast asks how the outcome would differ under specified alternatives. The three shapes are X←C→YX \leftarrow C \rightarrow Y for a confounder, X→M→YX \rightarrow M \rightarrow Y for a mediator, and X→S←YX \rightarrow S \leftarrow Y for a collider or selection variable. Random assignment breaks systematic links between assignment and pre-assignment causes over repeated randomizations, but chance can leave a particular pair of groups imbalanced.

If the contrast was missing from your answer, return to “Three targets of inference.” If the arrowheads were reversed, say each map aloud as a sentence about what causes what. If “identical” appeared in your randomization answer, replace it with “comparable in expectation” and name one possible chance imbalance.

Source Note
claims claim-inference-targets, claim-causal-contrast, claim-third-variable-roles, claim-randomization-scopesources source-causal-diagrams, source-natural-experiments, source-randomized-trials, source-mechanistic-evidence

The sources support the reconstructed distinctions, the three causal positions, and the difference between balance in expectation and exact balance in one assigned sample.

Repair the tempting rule

Misconception
id misconception-adjust-everythinglabel If a third variable is related to exposure and outcome, always adjust for it

Commit first. A dataset contains exposure XX, outcome YY, and a third variable ZZ associated with both. Should the analysis always adjust for ZZ?

Why “yes” fails. Association with both variables does not reveal ZZ's causal job. If ZZ is a pre-exposure common cause, adjustment may help block a confounding path. If ZZ carries part of the effect, adjustment removes part of the total-effect route. If ZZ is a common effect, adjustment or selection on it may open a noncausal path.

Better model. Put the question first, establish time order, draw the assumed arrows, and adjust only for a set of variables that blocks the relevant noncausal paths without blocking the effect of interest or opening a collider path. Even a sensible adjustment set cannot remove bias from common causes that were not measured well enough.

Recheck. In the randomized app study, retrieval sessions occur after assigned access and before the exam. If the target is the total effect of the offer, should retrieval sessions be treated as a routine confounder? Explain your arrow map.

Retrieval sessions are a proposed mediator, not a pre-offer common cause. Routine adjustment would change the question away from the total effect of the offer. A formal direct-effect or mediation question is possible, but it needs a separately specified target and additional assumptions.

Source Note
claims claim-third-variable-rolessources source-causal-diagrams

The causal-diagram source supports choosing adjustment from the assumed causal structure and target effect, including the risks of controlling for mediators or colliders and of leaving common-cause paths open.

Fade the support

Each case below is fictional and exists only to test the causal structure.

Completion problem: label the arrows

Across a workshop network, airborne dust CC influences whether technicians turn on a filter XX and also raises a particle-count measurement YY. Complete the map and conclusion:

XX ______ CC ______ YY. Therefore CC is a ______, and the crude filter–particle association is ______ by default.

Feedback. The map is X←C→YX \leftarrow C \rightarrow Y. Dust is a confounder, and the crude association is associational by default. If you drew X→CX \rightarrow C, check the timing: the background dust level affected the decision to use the filter.

Supported problem: separate a mechanism from a total effect

Researchers randomly assign a brief light pulse XX to cell cultures. The pulse changes a transcription factor MM; the factor then changes reporter fluorescence YY. They have experimental evidence for the first link and a separate perturbation study for the second.

  1. Draw the proposed mechanism.
  2. What does the combined evidence support?
  3. What would remain unjustified if the work used one cell line under one laboratory condition?

Feedback. The proposed chain is X→M→YX \rightarrow M \rightarrow Y. The two perturbations support a mechanism in which the transcription factor carries a causal link under the tested conditions. The evidence does not by itself justify the same pathway, effect size, or outcome in other cell types, organisms, doses, or environments. If your conclusion jumped directly to patients or populations, shrink it to the experimental system.

Independent problem: spot selection on a common effect

Machine age XX and an operator's maintenance skill YY both affect whether a machine enters a repair registry SS. An analyst studies only registered machines and finds that older machines tend to have more skilled operators.

Write a two-sentence audit: name SS's role, then explain why the selected-sample association need not exist in all machines.

Feedback. The registry is a collider on X→S←YX \rightarrow S \leftarrow Y. Restricting analysis to S=1S=1 can induce an association between machine age and operator skill because either cause can help a machine enter the registry. If you called the registry a confounder, ask whether it precedes and causes both XX and YY; it does not.

Source Note
claims claim-inference-targets, claim-third-variable-roles, claim-mechanism-evidencesources source-causal-diagrams, source-mechanistic-evidence

The sources support distinguishing an observed association from a causal chain, treating perturbation evidence as local to its tested system, and recognizing selection on a common effect as a route to noncausal association.

Two probes for rival explanations

A negative control deliberately substitutes an exposure or outcome that should not have the tested causal relation but should share important bias-producing conditions. If the supposedly impossible association appears, residual confounding, selection, or measurement error may still be present. A clean negative control does not prove that every bias is absent; the control works only if its own assumptions are credible.

Triangulation compares evidence from approaches chosen because their important potential biases differ and are expected to be largely unrelated. Convergence can strengthen confidence when the same wrong answer is unlikely to arise from those different biases. Repeating several statistical methods on the same confounded dataset is not automatically triangulation, and convergence is not proof.

Check
id check-negative-control-triangulation

An observational app analysis and its negative-control outcome both show similar associations with app use. What should happen to confidence in a causal app effect, and why? Then name one genuinely different approach that could contribute to triangulation.

Confidence should decrease because the negative-control association suggests a shared noncausal process. A randomized offer study, a defensible external allocation rule, or a mechanism experiment could add a different route of evidence, provided its main bias sources are not the same as the cohort's. The appropriate next step is to diagnose the shared bias, not to average the two associations.

Source Note
claims claim-negative-controls, claim-triangulationsources source-negative-controls, source-triangulation

The sources support using negative controls as bias detectors under stated assumptions and using triangulation only when the contributing approaches have meaningfully different key sources of potential bias.

scenes.title: Transfer the judgment to a laboratory system
scenes.contentHtml:
Transfer the judgment to a laboratory system

A university laboratory office has ten automated pipette-checking stations for forty introductory teaching labs. Researchers want to know whether receiving a station reduces liquid-transfer errors. This transfer case is fictional; its allocation, measurements, and results were created for this lesson.

The evidence arrives in three forms:

  1. Routine records: labs already using a station report fewer transfer errors than labs without one. Better-funded labs were more likely to buy stations and also had more staff training.
  2. Bench evidence: when researchers deliberately introduce a calibration fault under controlled conditions, the station detects the fault before transfer and blocks the next automated step.
  3. External allocation: among forty eligible applicants, the equipment office uses a documented computer lottery to allocate its ten stations. Researchers compare all labs according to lottery allocation, use the same blinded scoring procedure, and record outcomes for every lab.
Check
id check-transfer-laboratory

Without copying the app example's wording, prepare an evidence judgment that does all of the following:

  • states the association supported by the routine records;
  • maps one confounder and explains its direction;
  • states the mechanism supported by the bench evidence and its boundary;
  • decides whether the external allocation is a natural-experiment candidate and names two assignment facts to verify;
  • gives the strongest causal statement if the lottery and outcome procedures are verified;
  • names one reason the causal statement may not transport beyond the applicant labs; and
  • explains how the three evidence forms can be integrated without pretending that one design supplies another design's strength.

Attempt the complete judgment before reading the model response.

Model response

The routine records support an association: existing station use accompanies fewer recorded transfer errors. Funding is a plausible common cause because it can influence both purchasing a station and staff training, which can influence errors. The crude station–error difference therefore does not isolate a station effect.

The bench perturbation supports a mechanism segment: under the tested fault and bench conditions, the station detects the fault and prevents the next automated step. It does not establish how often that fault occurs in teaching labs, the total effect on all error types, or the effect in another setting.

The office lottery is a natural-experiment candidate because an external process, not the research team or lab preference, allocated access. Before giving it experiment-like weight, verify at least that every eligible applicant truly had the stated lottery chance, the allocation could not be manipulated or overridden, no other resource was bundled with a station, and outcome recording stayed complete and comparable. If those checks hold, the comparison can support this bounded statement:

Allocation of a pipette-checking station reduced the mean recorded transfer-error rate among the forty applicant labs during the study period, compared with no allocation.

The statement is about allocation, the applicant labs, the measured errors, and the study period. It may not transport to nonapplicant labs with different staff, workflows, equipment, or error patterns. Actual station use may also differ from allocation.

Together, the routine records show the real-setting pattern, the bench study supports how one detection step can work, and the verified lottery supports causal attribution for allocation. Their different weaknesses make convergence more informative than repetition of one analysis. Convergence would still not erase a shared measurement problem or justify a claim outside all three studies' scope.

Stress-test the conclusion

Change one fact at a time.

  • Managers could trade lottery positions before allocation. The assignment is no longer defensibly chance-based without further analysis; fall back to an observational association unless another design repairs the comparison.
  • Only labs that filed an incident report entered the dataset, and both station use and severe errors affected filing. Report inclusion is a collider; conditioning on it can induce selection bias.
  • The station flag is analyzed as though it were a pre-allocation confounder. The flag occurs after allocation and is part of the proposed mechanism, so routine adjustment changes the target.
  • A negative-control error category that the station cannot detect also falls sharply after allocation. Investigate a shared recording or co-intervention explanation before crediting the station.
  • A second study uses the same records and a different regression program. That may test software or model sensitivity, but it does not by itself create triangulation with unrelated bias sources.
Source Note
claims claim-inference-targets, claim-causal-contrast, claim-third-variable-roles, claim-randomization-scope, claim-mechanism-evidence, claim-natural-experiments, claim-negative-controls, claim-triangulationsources source-causal-diagrams, source-natural-experiments, source-randomized-trials, source-mechanistic-evidence, source-negative-controls, source-triangulation

The sources support calibrating each conclusion to assignment, causal structure, mechanism evidence, population, and measurement; treating external allocation as a causal opportunity only after its assignment process is defended; and using negative controls or genuinely different bias structures to probe the result. The laboratory case and all of its details are original fictional teaching material.

scenes.title: Make the strongest claim the design can carry
scenes.contentHtml:
Make the strongest claim the design can carry

Use this compact audit whenever a study summary invites causal language:

  1. Name the target. Is the claim about association, a mechanism, or a change under an intervention?
  2. Define the contrast. What exposure or assignment is compared with what alternative, for which outcome and population?
  3. Inspect assignment and time. Who or what set the exposure, and did the proposed cause precede the outcome?
  4. Map rival paths. Which pre-exposure common causes, reverse arrows, selection steps, measurement differences, or missing outcomes remain?
  5. Place intermediates correctly. Is a third variable a confounder to block, a mediator on the route of interest, or a collider to leave unconditioned for this question?
  6. State the boundary. Name the assignment, studied population, setting, outcome, period, and assumptions that limit the conclusion.
  7. Integrate without laundering. Mechanism studies, negative controls, natural experiments, and other approaches contribute their own evidence; none silently borrows another design's strengths.

An association can be scientifically useful without being causal. A mechanism can be well supported without establishing a population effect. A causal estimate can be credible within a randomized or defensibly assigned comparison while its mechanism or transport to another setting remains uncertain. Precision means saying exactly which of those achievements the evidence earns.

Source Note
claims claim-inference-targets, claim-causal-contrast, claim-third-variable-roles, claim-randomization-scope, claim-mechanism-evidence, claim-natural-experiments, claim-negative-controls, claim-triangulationsources source-causal-diagrams, source-natural-experiments, source-randomized-trials, source-mechanistic-evidence, source-negative-controls, source-triangulation

The sources support the audit's distinctions and boundaries: causal contrasts require defensible assignment or explicit identifying assumptions; third-variable handling depends on causal position; mechanism, negative-control, natural-experiment, and triangulation evidence each answer a different part of the causal case.

Mastery check

Complete the five mastery items without reopening earlier scenes. Keep the feedback closed until after you have made a genuine attempt.

The gate carries 15 points. Mastery requires at least 12 points and full credit on the final causal-attribution item, because an unsupported causal claim is the critical error for this outcome. A response below the threshold or an incomplete final item is “not yet”: follow the item-specific feedback, use the route below, and retry with the alternate context named in the answer reasoning. Retakes are untimed and unlimited.

Recovery route

  • If you confuse study labels, revisit Choosing a study design and identify assignment before returning.
  • If you state a causal conclusion too early, use a four-column table: observed contrast | assignment | rival path | strongest claim. Rework the app and laboratory cases one row at a time.
  • If third-variable roles blur together, redraw only the arrowheads. Say “common cause,” “on the route,” or “common effect” before using the technical label.
  • If you treat randomization as a guarantee, write “comparable in expectation” and add one implementation or population boundary.
  • If mechanism and effect blend together, write two sentences: “This evidence supports the process link…” and “This design supports/does not support the outcome contrast…”.

Accessibility and alternatives

All essential relationships appear in text as well as in the declarative diagram. You may answer every prompt by typing, speaking to a scribe, or drawing labelled arrows; no task depends on color, pointer movement, dragging, sound, animation, or a time limit. For the diagram, use the equivalent list: confounder X←C→YX \leftarrow C \rightarrow Y; mediator X→M→YX \rightarrow M \rightarrow Y; collider X→S←YX \rightarrow S \leftarrow Y. Tables preserve the same source order in narrow-screen, zoomed, screen-reader, and print views. If symbolic arrows are hard to interpret, replace them with the phrases “CC causes both,” “MM carries the route,” and “both cause SS.”

Delayed retrieval

  • Tomorrow: without notes, draw the three third-variable maps and write one sentence for the claim each design can support.
  • In one week: find a study summary from a non-clinical course topic. Before reading its conclusion, audit assignment, time order, one rival path, and the strongest warranted claim.
  • When you study bias and threats to validity: revisit the collider case and explain how selection into a sample can create an association.
  • When you study mechanistic and convergent evidence: return to the three app packets and ask what new evidence would strengthen the causal account without changing the original trial's population.

Do not use a published health headline as personal medical guidance. This lesson teaches evidence judgment, not diagnosis, treatment, or individual risk prediction.

Those sessions received only their science prompts and did not see this repository, Premed, the outcome ID, or the packaging request. assessment.title: Association, mechanism, and causal-attribution mastery check

items.prompt: Choose the only calibrated map. Study A measures current equipment age and current failure rate in one survey. Study B perturbs one molecular component and records the ordered downstream steps but does not measure a whole-organism outcome. Study C randomly assigns a laboratory procedure and measures the same later outcome in both assigned groups.

options.text: A supports association; B supports a bounded mechanism claim; C can support bounded causal attribution if implementation preserves the randomized comparison.
All three support causal attribution because each records a difference.
A supports mechanism; B proves a population effect; C supports association only.
Only C is informative, and it establishes the same effect in every population.

answer.summary: The three designs answer different questions: co-variation, process, and a bounded intervention contrast.
answer.reasoning: Award 1 point for classifying A as association and B as bounded mechanism evidence, and 1 point for allowing C a conditional, bounded causal interpretation rather than a universal one. If you chose another map, underline who assigned exposure and what outcome each study actually measured. On retry, classify a one-time habitat survey, a controlled enzyme-pathway perturbation, and a randomized materials test.
acceptableResponses: The option mapping A to association, B to a bounded mechanism claim, and C to conditional causal attribution.
commonErrors: Treating every measured difference as causal
Treating a process study as proof of a whole-system or population effect
Treating a randomized result as automatically universal

items.prompt: For a total-effect question about X on Y, interpret these assumed maps: (1) X ← Z1 → Y; (2) X → Z2 → Y; (3) X → Z3 ← Y. Name the role of Z1, Z2, and Z3, and state the default adjustment decision for each map.
items.stimulus: The arrows are causal assumptions and the variables occur in the order indicated.

answer.correct: Z1 is a confounder or pre-exposure common cause and should be blocked if it is correctly identified and measured. Z2 is a mediator on the causal route and should not be treated as a routine confounder when estimating the total effect. Z3 is a collider or common effect and should not be conditioned on for this simple total-effect question because doing so can induce association.
answer.summary: The arrowheads determine whether a third variable is a common cause, a route carrier, or a common effect.
answer.reasoning: Award 1 point for each correct role plus its total-effect adjustment consequence. If a label is correct but the adjustment is wrong, do not award that point: the assessment targets usable causal structure. On retry, replace Z1, Z2, and Z3 with background temperature, an intermediate sensor signal, and inclusion in an alarm-only sample.
acceptableResponses: Equivalent wording that blocks the confounder path, preserves the mediator for a total-effect question, and avoids conditioning on the collider.
commonErrors: Adjusting for every variable associated with both X and Y
Calling the mediator a confounder despite its post-exposure position
Calling the common effect a common cause

items.prompt: Four hundred eligible samples are randomly assigned to a new preparation procedure or the standard procedure. Outcomes are measured comparably for every assigned sample. The new-procedure group has a lower mean error count. Which conclusion is strongest without adding facts?

options.text: Assignment to the new procedure reduced the mean error count relative to standard assignment among these eligible samples under the study conditions; transport to other samples or implementation settings needs justification.
The two realized groups were identical in every characteristic except procedure, so every observed outcome difference must be exact.
Actually following the new procedure reduces errors for every sample, whether or not the assigned procedure was followed.
Random assignment cannot support causal inference, so only an association may be reported.

answer.summary: Randomization supports a bounded effect of assignment, not exact group identity, universal transport, or an effect of actual receipt under unknown adherence.
answer.reasoning: Award 2 points for selecting the bounded assignment-effect statement and 1 point for its explicit population or transport limit. Both features are present only in the correct option. On retry, evaluate random assignment of a microscope-cleaning protocol with the same outcome-measurement conditions.
acceptableResponses: The option attributing a lower mean error count to assignment in the eligible study sample while preserving the transport boundary.
commonErrors: Claiming exact realized balance from randomization
Changing an effect of assignment into an effect of actual use
Generalizing beyond the studied samples without support

items.prompt: A fictional observational study finds that laboratories using an optional calibration checklist have fewer recording errors. A negative-control outcome that the checklist cannot plausibly affect shows an association of similar size. Write the strongest current conclusion, explain what the negative control changes, and name a genuinely different kind of evidence that could strengthen or weaken the causal account.

answer.correct: Checklist use is associated with fewer recording errors, but the observational comparison does not isolate a checklist effect. The similar negative-control association lowers causal confidence because it signals residual confounding, selection, measurement, or another shared systematic process, assuming the control's no-effect and shared-bias conditions are credible. A randomized checklist assignment, a defensible external allocation rule, or a mechanism experiment with a distinct principal bias structure could contribute independent evidence; repeating regressions on the same data would not by itself be triangulation.
answer.summary: The negative control turns an untested rival explanation into evidence of possible systematic error, so the claim remains associational while a different evidence route is sought.
answer.reasoning: Award 1 point for keeping the current conclusion associational, 1 for explaining that the unexpected negative-control association lowers confidence under the control assumptions, and 1 for proposing a genuinely different evidence route rather than another model on the same data. On retry, use optional microscope-maintenance logs and an outcome the maintenance cannot affect.
acceptableResponses: Any answer that preserves association, interprets the negative control as a bias warning rather than proof, and proposes evidence with a meaningfully different key bias source.
commonErrors: Calling the negative-control association evidence that the checklist works
Treating a null or non-null control as conclusive without its assumptions
Calling several analyses of the same biased data independent triangulation

items.prompt: A regional science center has 12 sensor kits for 30 volunteer field teams and says it used a public random-number lottery to allocate them. Researchers did not control the lottery. All teams submit the same blinded sample-identification test. A bench study separately shows that the kit detects one specified sensor fault. Judge the association, mechanism, and causal-attribution claims: identify the design candidate, give two assignment or implementation checks, state the strongest causal conclusion if they pass, give one scope boundary, and explain what the bench study adds without overclaiming.
items.stimulus: The case is fictional and includes no unstated evidence about compliance, populations, or other fault types.

answer.correct: This is a natural-experiment candidate because an external lottery allocated kit access. Verify at least two facts such as genuine known lottery chances for every eligible team, no manipulation or overrides, no bundled co-intervention, complete analysis by original allocation, and comparable blinded outcome measurement. If those checks hold, allocation of a kit can be said to change the mean sample-identification result relative to no allocation among the 30 volunteer teams under the study conditions, with direction and magnitude taken from the observed comparison. The claim is about allocation and this applicant group, not actual use, every field team, every fault, or every setting. The bench study supports one detection link in a mechanism under its test conditions; it does not establish the field-outcome effect or transport it beyond the study.
answer.summary: A defended external lottery can support bounded causal attribution for allocation, while the bench study separately supports one mechanism link.
answer.reasoning: Award 1 point for identifying and justifying the natural-experiment candidate, 1 for two concrete allocation or implementation checks, 1 for a bounded effect-of-allocation statement plus a population or setting limit, and 1 for a calibrated mechanism statement that neither substitutes for nor inherits the field effect. Full credit on this item is required for lesson mastery. On retry, audit a municipal lottery for environmental data loggers with a separate controlled sensor-response study.
acceptableResponses: Any equivalent response that defends the external assignment, preserves an effect-of-allocation estimand and scope boundary, and keeps mechanism evidence distinct from the field outcome contrast.
commonErrors: Calling every outside allocation random without checking it
Claiming an effect of actual kit use rather than allocation
Generalizing from volunteer teams to every field team
Treating detection of one bench fault as proof of all field outcomes

criteria.description: Award 1 point only when Study A is classified as association and Study B as a bounded mechanism claim.
Award 1 point only when Study C is allowed a conditional causal interpretation without universal generalization.
Award 1 point for identifying Z1 as a common-cause confounder and blocking its path if correctly measured.
Award 1 point for identifying Z2 as a mediator and preserving it for the total-effect question.
Award 1 point for identifying Z3 as a collider and avoiding conditioning on it in the stated map.
Award 2 points only for attributing the mean outcome difference to assigned procedure under the stated comparison conditions.
Award 1 point only when the conclusion retains the eligible-sample or transport boundary.
Award 1 point for keeping the checklist result as association rather than causal attribution.
Award 1 point for explaining that the unexpected negative-control association signals possible shared systematic error under its assumptions.
Award 1 point for proposing an evidence route with a meaningfully different key bias source.
Award 1 point for identifying an external-lottery natural-experiment candidate rather than assuming a researcher-run trial.
Award 1 point for at least two concrete checks of lottery integrity, overrides, co-interventions, original allocation, follow-up, or outcome measurement.
Award 1 point for a causal statement about allocation that retains a volunteer-team, setting, outcome, or period boundary.
Award 1 point for treating the bench result as evidence for one mechanism link without using it as proof of the field outcome or universal transport.

claims.statement: Association evidence describes variables that vary together, mechanistic evidence supports the existence or details of a process connecting a proposed cause to an outcome, and causal attribution concerns an outcome contrast under specified alternatives.
claims.scope: Introductory distinction among descriptive association, evidence about a causal process, and a causal effect contrast.
claims.uncertainty: Philosophical and disciplinary accounts of mechanism and causation vary; the lesson uses a practical design-judgment distinction rather than claiming one universal theory of causation.

claims.statement: A causal effect contrasts outcomes under specified exposure or intervention alternatives, and observational data warrant causal interpretation only when design and explicit assumptions make the unobserved alternative comparison defensible.
claims.scope: Specified average contrasts in experimental and observational causal inference at an introductory level.
claims.uncertainty: The lesson does not teach formal identification, estimation, positivity, consistency, interference, or sensitivity-analysis mathematics.

claims.statement: A pre-exposure common cause can confound an exposure-outcome association, a mediator lies on a proposed causal path, and conditioning or selecting on a common effect can induce a noncausal association; adjustment therefore depends on causal position and the target effect.
claims.scope: Simple acyclic fork, chain, and collider structures used to reason about total-effect questions.
claims.uncertainty: Real causal systems may include feedback, time-varying confounding, measurement error, and effect modification that these three-node maps do not represent.

claims.statement: Random assignment makes assigned groups comparable in expectation rather than guaranteeing exact balance in one experiment and can identify a bounded effect of assignment when implementation and outcome measurement preserve the comparison; transport beyond the studied sample requires further justification.
claims.scope: Randomized comparisons and intention-to-treat effects of assignment in a defined study population.
claims.uncertainty: Specific estimands, nonadherence, missing outcomes, treatment spillover, chance imbalance, and precision require design-specific analysis beyond this lesson.

claims.statement: Mechanistic evidence can support the existence or details of entities, activities, and ordered links between a proposed cause and outcome, but it does not by itself estimate the total outcome effect in every target population.
claims.scope: Evidence about a causal process and its use alongside direct outcome comparisons.
claims.uncertainty: Mechanism terminology and the evidential weight assigned to mechanistic versus difference-making evidence vary across disciplines and causal questions.

claims.statement: A natural experiment studies exposure variation created outside researcher control, and its causal strength depends on a defensible assignment process, comparable outcome measurement, and explicit checks of alternative explanations.
claims.scope: External rules, events, policies, or allocation processes used as natural-experiment opportunities.
claims.uncertainty: Definitions range from broad externally caused exposure changes to narrower as-if-random allocation; the lesson requires the assignment process to be defended before causal language is used.

claims.statement: A negative-control exposure or outcome is chosen to lack the tested causal relation while sharing important bias-producing conditions, so an unexpected association can reveal residual confounding or other systematic error, but a null control does not prove all bias absent.
claims.scope: Negative-control logic as a diagnostic probe in observational or experimental research.
claims.uncertainty: A control is informative only when its no-effect and shared-bias assumptions are scientifically credible for the question.

claims.statement: Triangulation can strengthen causal confidence when results from approaches with meaningfully different and largely unrelated key bias sources converge, while inconsistency can identify assumptions needing further study; convergence is not proof.
claims.scope: Qualitative integration of different research approaches for one causal question.
claims.uncertainty: Bias sources may be shared or mischaracterized, and contributing studies may estimate different populations, timings, exposures, or outcomes.

terms.definition: A pattern in which measured values or categories of two variables differ together in a sampled dataset; the pattern alone does not specify why it occurs.
The intervention, condition, characteristic, or experience whose relationship with an outcome is being studied.
The response, event, state, or measurement a study compares across exposure or assignment conditions.
Evidence about the existence or details of the entities, activities, ordered changes, and conditions that connect a proposed cause to an outcome.
A conclusion that an outcome difference is due to a specified exposure, intervention, or assignment rather than to the alternative explanations left by the design.
A contrast between outcomes under two or more specified exposure or intervention alternatives for a defined population and period.
The explicit comparison of an outcome under one exposure or assignment with the outcome under a stated alternative.
The outcome that would occur for a unit under an alternative exposure or assignment, including an alternative not actually experienced by that unit at that time.
For the simple structures in this lesson, a pre-exposure common cause that opens a noncausal path between exposure and outcome.
Confounding that remains after analysis because relevant common causes were unmeasured, measured poorly, or controlled inadequately.
A direction in which the proposed outcome influences the proposed exposure, so the observed association may run opposite to the initial causal story.
A variable on a proposed causal route from exposure to outcome that carries some part of the effect under the stated model.
A variable that is a common effect of two other variables on a causal path; conditioning or selecting on it can induce an association between its causes.
Restricting, stratifying, matching, or statistically adjusting an analysis so comparisons are made within or weighted by values of another variable.
Allocation by a chance process with known assignment probabilities that makes pre-assignment causes comparable across groups in expectation.
The property that assignment does not systematically favor one group across repeated randomizations, even though chance imbalance can occur in one realized assignment.
A comparison that keeps units in their originally assigned groups regardless of whether they received or used the assigned intervention.
A study of exposure variation created by an external rule, event, policy, or allocation process whose assignment must be examined before experiment-like causal claims are made.
An exposure or outcome chosen to lack the tested causal relation while sharing important bias-producing conditions, so an unexpected association can signal systematic error.
Integration of results from approaches selected because their important potential biases differ and are assumed to be largely unrelated.

attributionHtml:
Attribution

Lesson identity and accountability

  • Lesson: PREM-SCI-007 — From correlation to a causal claim
  • Version and state: 0.1.0 / draft / pending review
  • Accountable principal: VSBDev (github:VSBDev), verified through the authenticated GitHub CLI session on 21 July 2026
  • Course-content license: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0)

VSBDev is accountable for accepting the scientific baseline, verifying sources, directing the editorial packager, and offering the original lesson pack under CC BY 4.0. The agent actor records below disclose material assistance; they do not transfer accountability away from VSBDev.

Blind Fable science authorship

The accepted science dossier contained thirteen fragments produced in thirteen isolated Claude CLI calls. Each call received only the science prompt reproduced in that dossier. The dossier states that no call received this repository, Premed, the graph outcome, or the editorial-packaging request. Each accepted call's machine-readable modelUsage contained claude-fable-5 and also reported claude-haiku-4-5-20251001; all Opus-routed outputs were excluded before the dossier was supplied to this run. The Claude CLI version was not included in the dossier and is therefore recorded as not supplied rather than guessed.

The lesson manifest preserves each accepted run as a separate actor entry within the schema instead of combining thirteen runs into a false single agent identity.

Fragment Claude session / run ID Exact science-prompt SHA-256
1 4eb4feb3-28c9-48af-b282-13d6ff6036f0 sha256:013402adf05935ae979e63acba1848ac1e60714a8bbec1962c17409345d41a86

4 30aef95a-473b-4b6a-add2-1ef2bd16c077 sha256:00eda732125ec5448bf9560035e5cb5e78c68f1a66fb41f8847fc3106ef0ad54
5 bb1f3eae-b37b-4f88-9b2d-79e4dc9c52f6 sha256:50c85c832fb04b2aef87265734855cd164dc1e420ab96c511ae8b6a111fb5908
6 d793cd68-3705-4d18-9b39-133fc23a7bb9 sha256:6f52df27f35e442207a3665c37b04db7f2ca2e85103114f841a8b3ccc9ce734b
7 43aa1117-1ead-4b22-b7b6-69115e70e4f3 sha256:b975d4302af05081e80a33aa954a9e47a1afaf07fc946c8158296e15261cd0cd
8 ac46eb18-f44b-4130-86ae-5b22171f5c71 sha256:267c30e20f50bab1d4479b64705b6eb30cbfc7598d80a490db1d9e3b75fbb77f
9 d1e9013a-f972-43ae-9029-a598c3a4095e sha256:d844640581d30505248301b1d41d67e6ed88e6c6f06a124ec20554640b2df66c
10 20769d6f-acc5-4460-bd76-c080f9daa722 sha256:ad5085440ae6a81448b3f06f699784ab9ef10f987a3deaa34c2916d0eef0503a
11 8115d4b0-45de-402a-90b4-f9ec2c07ce65 sha256:d70cba87a062cb6688f049fb856e6f32f4eecab1005f258296f0a0b751329364
12 12821e17-0234-4127-be95-24457c65a584 sha256:04d8cd6fcedb17a4df1cb28cc960276e401e14b336d60cd10bfb845accd5d588
13 d3898e27-15c2-4060-bd6b-81aee94b0c7f sha256:80383752d5ed4d0e6ec0a8f6d5026136abd1841145ed4485583ad99cc5daca82

The baseline covered association and intervention contrasts; confounding, reverse direction, mediators, and colliders; randomized and natural experiments; instrumental-variable assumptions; mechanism evidence; negative controls; and triangulation. Editorial verification retained the accurate introductory substance and corrected or narrowed overstatements. In particular, the lesson states balance in expectation rather than identical realized groups, distinguishes an effect of assignment from actual receipt, qualifies confounder adjustment, keeps mediator and collider handling tied to the target effect, and does not imply that a natural experiment or instrument is valid merely because it is labelled as such. Formal instrumental-variable estimation and mediation decomposition were excluded from the learner scope rather than taught without their full assumptions.

Editorial and pedagogical packaging assistance

Material source verification, evidence mapping, learning design, prose integration, original examples, assessment design, accessibility work, rights records, and Lesson Format v1 packaging used:

  • System: Codex
  • Provider: OpenAI
  • Model: gpt-5.6-sol
  • Version: Codex CLI 0.144.6
  • Run ID: 1c340f61-8e78-4c1f-98a1-a6c424db92fc
  • Exact discloseable material-prompt digest: sha256:3ba75d15bc057164820d75ce1755421cb557a3206bcb14e400a0a7aaf50820d2

The Codex digest was supplied through EK_PACKAGER_PROMPT_SHA256 and covers the exact discloseable editorial-packaging task prompt for this run. It does not claim to cover hidden OpenAI provider or system instructions. Codex is recorded as material editorial and pedagogical assistance, not as the blind science author.

Original synthesis and source use

The opening, flashcard-app case, causal-role diagram, workshop and cell-culture practice, laboratory-station transfer, sensor-kit assessment, feedback, remediation, accessibility equivalents, instructional sequence, and all fictional data are original material created for this pack. The accepted Fable fragments supplied a science-only baseline; the packager independently verified consequential claims and constructed a new learner-facing explanation rather than preserving the dossier's wording or sequence.

Every source in references.json was used only as a factual reference. No source wording, distinctive example, question, table, figure, media, or dataset was copied, translated, adapted, or redistributed. Source licenses are recorded for transparency but are not relied on to reuse source expression.

Source-access and rights attestation

Before substantive source access on 21 July 2026, the NCBI website/data policies and robots route were checked. The generic PMC article route was not used because that robots route disallows it for generic agents. The six articles were read through their public NCBI BioC REST endpoints, which were not disallowed, at a compliant low request rate; public PubMed metadata was used where available. No login, paywall, rate limit, or technical control was bypassed, and no source was marked human-only. Exact access notes, stable PubMed URLs, DOIs, PMCID identifiers, locators, displayed licenses, factual-use bases, and limitations are recorded in references.json.

No third-party image, diagram, audio, video, software, or dataset is included. The declarative diagram is original course content and is offered under the pack's CC BY 4.0 license.

Candidate boundary

This is an author-stage draft with every claim marked pending-review. It contains no review or adjudication artifact and makes no claim of approval, publication, course coverage, learner effectiveness, clinical sufficiency, academic credit, admission eligibility, or merge readiness.
